items.foco: Most common cause of inspiratory stridor
items.title: Most common cause of inspiratory stridor
items.contentHtml:
Most common cause of inspiratory stridor

  • Congenital laryngomalacia is the most common etiology of inspiratory stridor in neonates and infants, representing up to 60-70% of cases of congenital stridor.
  • Stridor is low-pitched, intermittent, and worsens with crying, feeding, or supine position.
  • It must be differentiated from other causes such as vocal cord paralysis, subglottic stenosis or subglottic hemangioma.
  • The diagnosis is confirmed by flexible nasolaryngoscopy which shows inspiratory supraglottic collapse.

items.title: Inspiratory stridor from birth
items.contentHtml:
Inspiratory stridor from birth

  • Stridor in congenital laryngomalacia is usually present from the first hours or days of life, although it sometimes becomes evident after discharge.
  • It is a pure inspiratory stridor, of a mild to moderate nature, which may be accompanied by mild intercostal retractions.
  • The presence of stridor from birth requires ruling out other congenital causes such as laryngeal atresia, congenital subglottic stenosis or laryngeal cysts.
  • Flexible nasolaryngoscopy is the gold standard test to differentiate these entities.

items.foco: Peak of maximum incidence at 2 weeks
items.title: Peak of maximum incidence at 2 weeks
items.contentHtml:
Peak of maximum incidence at 2 weeks

  • The peak of maximum incidence of stridor due to congenital laryngomalacia occurs around 2 weeks of life, coinciding with the increase in airflow and inspiratory force.
  • The symptoms usually worsen progressively until the sixth month and then improve spontaneously.
  • This temporal pattern helps to differentiate it from fixed causes. such as subglottic stenosis, where stridor is constant from birth.
  • Spontaneous resolution before the age of 2 years is the rule in more than 90% of cases.

items.title: Supraglottic collapse during inspiration
items.contentHtml:
Supraglottic collapse during inspiration

  • The key pathophysiology is supraglottic collapse during inspiration due to excessive flaccidity of the upper laryngeal structures.
  • Inspiratory drag collapses the epiglottis and aryepiglottic folds towards the glottis, partially obstructing the airway.
  • This mechanism explains why the Stridor is inspiratory and not expiratory, unlike tracheal or bronchial pathologies.
  • Flexible nasolaryngoscopy allows visualization of dynamic collapse in real time during spontaneous breathing.

items.title: Omega-shaped or accordion-shaped epiglottis
items.contentHtml:
Omega-shaped or accordion-shaped epiglottis

  • A characteristic finding in flexible nasolaryngoscopy is the omega-shaped (Ω) or accordion-shaped epiglottis, which folds back during inspiration.
  • The epiglottis appears elongated, tubular and with the lateral edges folded medially, which contributes to collapse supraglottic.
  • This appearance is frequently associated with fluttering of redundant arytenoid bands that prolapse into the glottis.
  • The classification of laryngomalacia (type 1, 2, 3) is based on the predominant anatomical structure that collapses.

items.title: Flapping of arytenoid bands
items.contentHtml:
Arytenoid band flaring

  • Arytenoid band flaring refers to the inspiratory prolapse of the redundant mucosa of the arytenoids and aryepiglottic folds into the glottis.
  • It is a common component of congenital laryngomalacia and may be the main cause of collapse in the type variety 2.
  • During inspiration, the bands are aspirated towards the laryngeal inlet, generating severe stridor and retractions.
  • Flexible nasolaryngoscopy confirms the finding by observing the redundant arytenoid mucosa that partially occludes the lumen.

items.title: Diagnosis confirmed with flexible nasolaryngoscopy
items.contentHtml:
Confirmed diagnosis with flexible nasolaryngoscopy

  • Flexible nasolaryngoscopy is the gold standard for the diagnosis of congenital laryngomalacia; allows visualization of dynamic supraglottic collapse during inspiration.
  • It is performed without sedation or with minimal sedation so as not to abolish the tone of the airway, which could mask the collapse.
  • Typical findings include omega-shaped epiglottis, arytenoid prolapse and shortening of the aryepiglottic folds.
  • If nasolaryngoscopy is inconclusive, it can be complemented with direct laryngoscopy under anesthesia to rule out other injuries.

items.foco: Self-limited and resolves before 2 years of age
items.title: Self-limited and resolves before 2 years of age
items.contentHtml:
Self-limited and resolves before the age of 2 years

  • Congenital laryngomalacia is a self-limited condition that resolves spontaneously in more than 90% of cases before 2 years of age.
  • Improvement usually begins after the sixth month of life, with gradual disappearance of stridor and retractions.
  • Initial management is conservative: observation, prone or semi-sitting position, and treatment of associated gastroesophageal reflux.
  • No specific pharmacological treatment is required for laryngomalacia itself, only symptomatic support.

items.title: Surgical indication: severe respiratory distress
items.contentHtml:
Surgical indication: severe respiratory distress

  • Surgery is indicated in cases of severe respiratory distress which includes: cyanosis, obstructive apneas, severe retractions, documented hypoxemia or cor pulmonale.
  • Other indications are failure of weight loss or poor weight gain) and severe feeding difficulties (aspirations, coughing during feeding). the majority evolve favorably with conservative management.

items.title: Surgery: laser supraglottoplasty
items.contentHtml:
Surgery: laser supraglottoplasty

  • Laser supraglottoplasty (CO₂ or diode laser) is the standard surgical technique for severe congenital laryngomalacia.
  • It consists of the resection of the redundant mucosa of the arytenoids, the aryepiglottic folds and, if necessary, necessary, the epiglottis in omega.
  • It is performed under general anesthesia with intubation or jet ventilation; The goal is to widen the laryngeal inlet and avoid inspiratory collapse.
  • Complications include laryngeal edema, supraglottic stenosis, and transient aspiration; The success rate is more than 90%.

items.title: Frequent association with gastroesophageal reflux
items.contentHtml:
Frequent association with gastroesophageal reflux

  • There is a frequent association with gastroesophageal reflux (GER) in up to 50-80% of children with congenital laryngomalacia.
  • GER aggravates laryngeal inflammation and supraglottic edema, worsening stridor and retractions.
  • Empiric treatment with proton pump inhibitors (e.g. omeprazole 1-2 mg/kg/day) or antacids is recommended during the observation period.
  • Control of GER may reduce the need for surgery by reducing laryngeal irritation and edema.

items.foco: Rule out other causes of stridor with laryngoscopy
items.title: Rule out other causes of stridor with laryngoscopy
items.contentHtml:
Rule out other causes of stridor with laryngoscopy

  • In the case of congenital inspiratory stridor, it is mandatory to rule out other causes by direct laryngoscopy if flexible nasolaryngoscopy is inconclusive.
  • Key differential diagnoses: vocal cord paralysis (unilateral or bilateral), stenosis subglottic cysts (congenital or acquired), subglottic hemangioma, laryngeal cysts and laryngeal membrane. Airway endoscopy (bronchoscopy) may also be necessary to rule out tracheal or bronchial pathology.
